entry.title: Federating, Replicating, and Caching Remote Data in CAP — Part 1
entry.content:
Last year and this year at reCAP I had the honor and opportunity to give two talks on CAP performance optimization with data replication and caching. While I was able to introduce the core ideas, 20-minute talks are way too short to discuss everything that matters in practice. So, here are some more details I want to share:
Almost every CAP application on BTP sooner or later needs data that lives somewhere else. An S/4HANA system, a SuccessFactors instance, another CAP service, some REST API. And every team ends up answering the same questions: Do we fetch it live? Do we copy it? Do we cache it? And in which project have we used the required handler code we need to copy over? 😄

What I'll present here didn't emerge in a vacuum. It's been inspired by many great resources on the topic: SAP's Service Integration guide, the xtravels reference sample (which consumes xflights), the Risk Management ext-service branch, Kai Niklas's CAP Remote Services walkthrough, and Gregor Wolf's cap-replication-demo.

This two-part series is my attempt to share the full picture:

  • Part 1 (this post) covers the theory: the terminology, what CAP provides out of the box, where the gaps are, and how a combined set of open-source plugins closes them with small code examples along the way.
  • Part 2 is a live walkthrough: we set up a small demo application (available on GitHub) that federates, replicates, and caches remote data, step by step.

Terminology

Before diving in, it's important to straighten out the terms as they overlap with terms the CAP team uses in the Service Integration guide. The vocabulary below is what the rest of this series uses.

Federation

Federation is the umbrella term and here means: integrating data from various sources into your CAP application's data model and exposing it as a unified interface. Regardless of where the data physically comes from and regardless of whether queries run live against the remote or against a local copy. This follows the broader industry meaning ("make multiple data sources appear as one"). Please note that CAP's own Data Federation guide uses the term more narrowly, as it there specifically means copying remote data into the local database so it can be joined with local data in SQL. In this series, that narrower meaning is called replication, and federation is the umbrella above all strategies.

Delegation

Delegation is a strategy within federation: incoming requests are live-forwarded (delegated) to the remote service at request time. The CAP application does not persist the remote data in its local model, it acts as a transparent proxy, translating each query on the way out and each response on the way back. This includes OData-specific mechanics like $expand and navigation, where local data and remote data need to be combined at runtime into a single response for the client.
Delegation shines when data must be fresh (think value helps, stock levels, prices) and hurts when the remote is slow, rate-limited or gets hit once per row.

Replication

Replication means the data is physically copied from the remote source into the local database and integrated directly into the application's data model. The copy runs as a separate, scheduled process, (mostly) decoupled from incoming requests, so the application can serve the data as if it were local. Well... it actually is. Full SQL, joins with your own entities, analytics, no network hop per request are the benefits. The trade-off is the flip side of delegation: data is as fresh as the last sync and someone has to run that sync reliably. You potentially need scheduling, delta detection, retries, monitoring and more.

Caching

Caching is deliberately not a third strategy. It's an option on top of either one: a delegated entity can cache responses to avoid hammering the remote with identical queries and even a replicated entity can cache expensive local aggregations. Keeping caching orthogonal avoids a classic design mistake,  a cache that quietly becomes an unmanaged replica.

To summarize:

TermWhat happens to the dataWhen queries hit the remote
FederationUmbrella: remote data becomes part of your app's modeldepends on strategy
Delegationstays remote, proxied liveevery request (unless cached)
Replicationcopied into the local DB on a schedulenever at request time
Cachingoption on either strategyon cache miss

What CAP provides out of the box and what's missing

CAP's support for consuming remote services is genuinely good and the Service Integration guide ('Calesi' — CAP-level Service Interfaces) has been updated a lot over the last year and is a must to read. It explains all the required building blocks:

You import the external service definition and CAP mocks it locally out of the box. You declare a consumption view as a plain CDS projection on the remote entity:

using { API_BUSINESS_PARTNER as remote } from './external/API_BUSINESS_PARTNER';

entity Customers as projection on remote.A_BusinessPartner {
    key BusinessPartner   as ID,
        BusinessPartnerFullName as name
};

A client's $filter=name eq 'ACME' is translated to BusinessPartnerFullName eq 'ACME' on the remote. Renames, $select restriction, all handled by CAP out of the box. 

Replication is similarly approachable at first: add local persistence to the projection (@CDS.persistence.table), then page through the remote and upsert what it returns:

async function syncCustomers() {
    const remote = await cds.connect.to('API_BUSINESS_PARTNER')
    let page, offset = 0
    do {
        page = await remote.run(SELECT.from('A_BusinessPartner').limit(1000, offset))
        await UPSERT.into('ReplicatedCustomers').entries(page.map(mapToLocal))
        offset += 1000
    } while (page.length === 1000)
}

cds.spawn({ every: 600000 }, syncCustomers) // sync every 10 minutes

This is roughly the pattern SAP's own xtravels sample implements in srv/data-federation.js, and what Gregor Wolf's cap-replication-demo shows end-to-end against the S/4HANA Business Partner API.

So what's the problem? Well... the problem is everything around those lines:

For delegation:

  • Cross-service $expand. The added one-liner to delegate incoming requests works until a client expands from a local entity into the remote one (or the other way around). Now you're splitting the query, running both halves, and stitching results back together, for every affected entity and in both directions. The Service Integration guide shows how; it's real code, per entity.
  • Navigation and filters across the boundary. $filter=customer/name eq 'ACME' on a local entity with an association to remote data needs the same manual splitting and some more advanced custom handler coding.
  • Writes. Forwarding CUD requests means translating payloads through the renames too and deciding, per entity and operation, whether writes should be allowed at all.
  • Resilience. The remote will be slow or down at some point. Without timeouts, retries, and ideally caching, its problems become your problems, live and per request.

For replication:

  • Scheduling. Something has to trigger the sync. cds.spawn, a job scheduler, a heartbeat endpoint. And while CAP provides the tooling, per project someone has to build this.
  • Delta detection. Full loads don't scale. Tracking a high-watermark (modifiedAt, a key, an OData V2 date/time-field pair) is state you now own.
  • Retry with backoff. A flaky network at 3 a.m. shouldn't leave you with a half-synced table. And a 401 should not be retried the same way as a 503.
  • Concurrency. Two app instances, one schedule. Who wins? You need a guard or you get double syncs.
  • Idempotency. UPSERT, never SELECT-then-INSERT, or replays create duplicates.
  • Observability. When did the last sync run? How many records? Did it fail? Without a tracker and some management UI/API, the answer is "check the logs."

And the biggest one: it's not a generic approach

  • Nothing of what you build here is reusable. The delegate handler, the sync loop, the scheduler, the retry logic. All of it is written per entity and per project. The next federated entity means another handler,  the next project means the same patterns and the same coding all over again.

None of this is a criticism of CAP itself as it provides all the tools to stitch together your own integration logic. And that's a deliberate design choice. Data federation is varied and highly use-case dependent. A full-blown federation framework inside the CAP core would not be a good idea. But between "all the primitives are there" and "assemble them yourself, every time" sits a gap for something that removes the repetition without giving up the flexibility. Well... if you keep reading, I might have good news for you. 😄

Connecting the dots with a combined set of plugins

This is where a set of open-source plugins comes in.

 

  • cds-data-federation
    The annotation layer: @federation.delegate and @federation.replicate on consumption views
  • cds-data-pipeline
    The engine underneath replication: scheduled, traceable READ → MAP → WRITE jobs with retry, delta tracking, and a management API
  • cds-caching
    Optional response caching on either strategy

The core principle that everything follows from:

The consumption view is the federation contract. The CDS projection declares the schema and which fields, what shape, what renames. The @federation.* annotation declares the runtime behavior strategy, what to delegate or replicate, optional cache, opt-in writes.

You already model consumption views today as per official documentation, they are the initial projection. The plugin just reads what's there and adds logic based on your chosen federation strategy.

Delegation, declaratively

When an entity is annotated as @federation.delegate, the plugin will forward all incoming requests to the remote service. Before, it infers the source service and entity, the projected columns (which become the $select upper bound on every remote call as fields you don't project are never fetched) and the bidirectional rename mapping. 

using { API_BUSINESS_PARTNER as remote } from './external/API_BUSINESS_PARTNER';

@federation.delegate
entity Customers as projection on remote.A_BusinessPartner {
    key BusinessPartner          as ID,
        BusinessPartnerFullName  as name,
        Industry                 as industry
};

On top of what CAP's query translation already does, the plugin also handles the parts you'd otherwise code by hand: cross-service $expand in both directions (local → remote and remote → local, including cross-provider cases), navigation filters across the boundary, and static where clauses from the projection.

Caching on top

As mentioned before, caching is not a third strategy as it's an option on the annotation, and it comes in two flavors, that are relevant in delegation scenarios:

The response cache targets the "same query, hundreds of times" pattern: responses are cached by their query signature via cds-caching, with TTL and tag-based invalidation. Only identical queries (sharing the same CQN query) hit the cache; everything else still goes to the remote.

@federation.delegate: { cache: { strategy: 'response', ttl: 30000 } }
entity Customers as projection on remote.A_BusinessPartner { ... };

The entity cache goes further: instead of caching individual responses, the plugin pulls a full snapshot of the remote entity into a local SQLite table living right beside your application. That's the same pattern the replication cache from the CAP-JS-Community common plugin uses.
Internally for the snapshot pull, cds-data-pipeline is used under the hood (more on that engine in a moment). Any query against the annotated entity, including filters, sorting, aggregations is served from the entity cache... until the TTL expires. Only one remote read per TTL window is sent to the remote service with arbitrary local queries in between:

 

@federation.delegate: { cache: { strategy: 'entity', ttl: 3600000 } }
entity Customers as projection on remote.A_BusinessPartner { ... };

Replication, declaratively with a pipeline engine underneath

The second strategy is physically moving the data from the remote service into the local database. This can be done via the exact same projection, but a slightly different annotation:

@federation.replicate: { schedule: 600000, mode: 'delta', preload: true }
entity ReplicatedCustomers as projection on remote.A_BusinessPartner {
    key BusinessPartner          as ID,
        BusinessPartnerFullName  as name,
        Industry                 as industry
};

This is where the second plugin enters the picture. cds-data-federation doesn't implement syncing itself, it binds every @federation.replicate entity to a pipeline on cds-data-pipeline, the engine package. A pipeline a simple (potentially scheduled) READ → MAP → WRITE job between exactly one source and one target CAP service. The engine plugin also contributes precisely the list from the "missing" section above:

  • Scheduling (schedule, plus preload for an initial load at startup)
  • Delta detection with configurable high-watermark strategies (timestamp, key, OData V2 date/time fields)
  • Retry with exponential backoff — and a sane default policy: 4xx errors are not retried, everything else is
  • Concurrency guard via optimistic locking, so two app instances never run the same sync twice
  • Idempotent writes via UPSERT
  • Streaming — large datasets are paged and streamed in batches, never held fully in memory
  • A tracker and management OData service at /pipeline: every pipeline, every run, record counts, durations, errors — plus actions to trigger or flush a sync manually

While up until now everything just was annotations, replication also has support for more complex scenarios. If you need to hook into a sync to enrich records mid-flight, the engine emits CAP-style events for each phase that you can hook into to apply your custom logic. 

 

const pipelines = await cds.connect.to('data-pipeline')
pipelines.before('PIPELINE.MAP', 'ReplicatedCustomers', ({ records }) => {
    for (const r of records) r.industry ??= 'UNKNOWN'
})

And because the engine is an independent package, it's also usable without any annotation. CallingaddPipeline({ source, target, schedule }) registers a sync programmatically, for cases that aren't remote-service consumption views at all. For this series, though, think of it as the machinery that makes @federation.replicate production-grade.

Finally, the plugin also provides a UI and OData service to manage the pipelines, start and observe runs and get an overview of the replication "landscape" within your application.

Choosing a strategy

There is no golden rule for data federation, as the strategy is highly depending on various factors. My rule of thumb after having built both ways many times:

  • Delegate when freshness beats latency: value helps, transactional lookups, data that changes constantly, low request volume.
  • Delegate + cache when the same queries repeat: dashboards, dropdowns, anything read-heavy where 30 seconds of staleness is fine.
  • Replicate when you need SQL: joins with local data, aggregations, analytics, high request volume or a remote service you don't want in your request path at all.

Since the strategy is just an annotation on the consumption view, switching between them later is a one-line change. The schema contract stays identical. 

When this is not the right tooling

For completeness: this is application-layer integration and the CAP app owns the contract. If your joins should disappear into the database, HANA synonyms or Smart Data Access are the better layer. If you're building an enterprise-wide data fabric, that's SAP Business Data Cloud / Datasphere territory. And if the integration itself (transformations, B2B, routing) is the deliverable, that's Integration Suite. The comparison matrix in the docs walks through the alternatives row by row.

What's next

That's the theory. In Part 2 we get hands-on: a small demo application (source will be on GitHub) that consumes a remote CAP service, where we build up delegation, caching, and replication step by step, including the pipeline monitor and... since everything interesting needs to have AI these days, we will also also integrate CAP's newly introduced mcp annotation and talk to our federated API directly from Claude..

Regarding the plugins: Any feedback, questions and war stories from your own integration projects are very welcome. Again, data federation is multi-faceted. This is my idea how to tackle this on the back of the mighty features CAP provides. If you have an idea what's missing, how to make things even more flexible, please reach out to me on social media and let's discuss.  

And lastly, if you like the ideas and want to learn more, there is a dedicated documentation portal to read more about all the features from the various plugins... and even more links you can take a look into:


Documentation portalhttps://mikezaschka.github.io/cds-data/
cds-data-federation on npmhttps://www.npmjs.com/package/cds-data-federation
cds-data-pipeline on npmhttps://www.npmjs.com/package/cds-data-pipeline
cds-cachinghttps://github.com/mikezaschka/cds-caching
Source repohttps://github.com/mikezaschka/cds-data
CAP Service Integration guidehttps://cap.cloud.sap/docs/guides/integration/calesi
CAP Data Federation guidehttps://cap.cloud.sap/docs/guides/integration/data-federation

entry.title: Re: MCP Gateway in SAP Integration Suite: Your APIs, Ready for the Age of Agents
entry.content:
Great post, especially the section on MCP being a connectivity protocol, not a semantic layer. Yes!

The write-operation risk you mention is the one I take seriously: for reads, bad tool design costs tokens, for writes, it costs real business documents.

I'd love to see one more ingredient on top of gateway governance: a propose-then-approve step, where the agent suggests the transaction and a human or even a good ol' deterministic rule releases it before anything posts.

Will the upcoming deep dives cover recommended patterns for write tools? Would be grand!

entry.title: Re: Roles in GRC returning from Production to Development...
entry.content:
Hello Experts!

I encountered this same "issue" but, through my research, I learned that this is normal BRM behavior. Whenever an adjustment to a profile begins, the system changes the function's status to "Development" as the initial adjustments are performed in the development environment. If you follow the BRM steps through to completion, the system will revert the status to "Production."

Parameter 3005 only applies if you update the role and owners using the Role Mass Maintenance tool instead of single role maintenance.


Note: If you generate the profile in mass, the system will not recognize the process as complete and will keep the status as "Development" requiring you to change it manually.

Note2: If role updates are performed using the mass update tool, this status does not change.
Apply "NO" for Resetting: Once you are in Role Mass Maintenance and choose the role, ensure you set the parameter 3005 value to NO so your methodology and status are preserved.

Best Regards!

#GRC #Access Control #Role Management

entry.title: SAP Smart Form – eWay Bill Number Missing in PDF (Intermittent Issue)
entry.content:
SAP Smart Form – eWay Bill Number Missing in PDF (Intermittent Issue)

Hi Experts,

I am facing an intermittent issue in SAP Smart Forms related to eWay Bill printing for a production output type (ZPID).

Scenario

The invoice is created successfully.

The eWay Bill is generated and stored in the database.

During debugging, the eWay Bill number is available in the Smart Form Initialization.

The value is also present in the OTF output.

There are no conditions on the Smart Form window or text node that suppress the field.

However, the final PDF displays the eWay Bill number as blank for some invoices.

The issue is intermittent; other invoices print correctly.

Observations

The Smart Form generates the OTF with the correct eWay Bill value.

Since the value exists in the OTF, it appears that the Smart Form generation is successful.

The issue seems to occur only in the final PDF output.

Timing Analysis

Working case:

Invoice creation: 10:49:38

eWay Bill generation: 10:51:00

Delay: 1 minute 22 seconds

Negative case 1:

Invoice creation: 23:10:35

eWay Bill generation: 23:15:00

Delay: 4 minutes 25 seconds

Negative case 2:

Invoice creation: 12:04:13

eWay Bill generation: 12:09:00

Delay: 4 minutes 47 seconds

Both negative cases show a similar delay of around 5 minutes, while the working case has only about 1 minute 22 seconds delay.

Our team also noted that the eWay Bill response is updated by a background job running periodically, and one suggestion was to temporarily increase the job frequency to see whether the number of intermittent issues decreases.

My Questions

If the eWay Bill value is already present in the OTF, what could cause it to disappear in the final PDF?

Can digitally signed Smart Forms or the PDF generation process use an older snapshot of the data instead of the current OTF?

Has anyone seen a similar issue where OTF contains the correct value but the generated PDF is blank?

Besides timing, what other areas should I investigate (spool processing, digital signature, PDF conversion, output processing, etc.)?

Any suggestions or similar experiences would be greatly appreciated.

Antonio Maradiaga
Holla, SAP developers. In case you missed it, the MCP gateway in SAP Integration Suite is now generally available. API centric integration is here. It is a new paradigm to how we create our API artifacts and MCP servers on the new Integration cell runtime. Think of it as designing the API and its mediation logic in one place. With the MCP gateway, we can turn our existing APIs into tools for AI agents with authentication, rate limiting, and traceability.

Antonio Maradiaga

Also, Ashutosh published a couple of blog posts in SAP community that works you end to end on how to build and deploy your first API on integration self, and then how to govern it through the Developer Hub. Thomas Jung
And speaking of reCAP and all things CAP related. I wanted to point you towards the CDS-Caching project by Mike Zaschka. It's in its 2.0 version. He's just updated it for CAP 10. I can tell you I've already used it in a CAP 10 project. Found it very handy. I'm using it to catch some remote data that's frequently read.

entry.title: Re: 咨询S4 Public Cloud 并行线系统是否支持增加自定义字段和自定义逻辑以及会否影响已经上线的公司
entry.content:
Dear @JuliaLu,

Yes, with restrictions. The parallel line supports business configuration and client-dependent key-user extensibility but not every extensibility object can be maintained independently there

The first screenshot shows expected behavior. SAP confirms that Custom Fields are client-independent they must be created in the main line, after which they are automatically available in the parallel line. They cannot be created separately in the parallel line. The same restriction applies to custom logic. Key user BAdI implementations and behavior extensions must be implemented via the Custom Logic app in the main line customizing tenant

For the Custom CDS Views screen, editability depends on whether SAP classifies the specific extensibility item type as client-dependent. This should be checked in the Extensibility Settings app. Only supported client dependent items can be created or changed independently in the parallel line

The partner can configure the new company codes in the parallel line without affecting production until the changes are merged and transported. After transport, shared or global configuration and extensibility objects can potentially affect existing companies. Therefore, company code restrictions should be included in custom logic where the BAdI provides the relevant organizational data, followed by regression testing

More details below:

SAP Help – Key User Extensibility in the Parallel Line

SAP KBA 3433477 – Best Practices for Key User Extensibility in Parallel Line

SAP KBA 3248190 – Items of Type XXX Must Not Be Edited in This Tenant

SAP KBA 3342103 – Key User Extensibility Apps Are Not Available or Read Only

SAP Help – Merge: Synchronize the Configurations of Your Parallel Line with Your Main Line

SAP KBA - 3670653 - Error "Items of type Custom Logic: XXX must not be edited in this tenant" - SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Implementing IFRS S1 and S2 Sustainability Disclosure Standards with End‑to‑End Reporting Flows
entry.content:
Introduction

The IFRS® Sustainability Disclosure Standards, IFRS S1 (General Requirements for Disclosure of Sustainability-Related Financial Information) and IFRS S2 (Climate-Related Disclosures), issued in June 2023 and effective for annual reporting periods beginning on or after 1 January 2024, are a common baseline for sustainability-related financial disclosure. As a growing number of jurisdictions around the world are adopting these standards, organizations are increasingly expected to report on climate-related risks and opportunities comparable to those applied in financial reporting. Sustainability reporting is dynamic by design as it considers material impacts, risks, and opportunities. Investors, for example, can now look to sustainability KPIs as signals to move from mostly stewardship non-financial reporting to financial-grade sustainability analysis for sustainability strategic business decisions.

For organizations reporting under the European Sustainability Reporting Standards (ESRS) or implementing IFRS Sustainability Disclosure Standards, reporting requirements are increasingly interconnected across frameworks. Consequently, interoperability is considered a critical enabler for organizations to align sustainability data across multiple regulatory and voluntary standards. SAP Sustainability solutions adopt interoperability as a characteristic for companies reporting under multiple frameworks, which can increasingly consolidate their sustainability data management rather than duplicating effort across disconnected processes.

End-to-end sustainability reporting requires an auditable chain from operational cause through identification, collection, calculation, analysis, and disclosure. Point tools own a slice of that chain, while SAP owns the whole of it. SAP Sustainability Control Tower and SAP Sustainability Footprint Management together enable an end‑to‑end IFRS S1 and S2 sustainability reporting flow, including carbon footprint calculation, and climate- and sustainability-related data acquisition through to governed disclosures. SAP Sustainability Control Tower now provides SAP‑delivered IFRS S1 and S2 data points as master content, so organizations can manage IFRS S1 and S2 disclosures within the same governance framework they already use for ESRS reporting, while SAP Sustainability Footprint Management supplies the underlying emissions and activity data needed for IFRS S2 climate‑related metrics. This post walks through the integrated flow from initial setup and data integration through to AI‑assisted report creation.

Initial Setup and Master Data

The starting point for IFRS S1 and S2 reporting in SAP Sustainability Control Tower is the Manage Master Data application. Here, organizations configure the foundational structures that underpin their sustainability disclosures, including reporting structures such as legal entities, business locations, and organizational units, as well as the associated hierarchies that define the reporting boundary.

Now, the reporting requirements under IFRS S1 and S2, version 2023, are provided by SAP in the “Reporting Standard”, “Reporting Standard Version,” and “Data Points” dimensions in the Manage Master Data App as of July 1st, 2026.

For IFRS reporting specifically, the same organizational reporting structure used to support ESRS disclosures is reused. This means organizations already configured for CSRD reporting do not need to rebuild their hierarchy levels, fiscal year variants, and organizational mappings, providing a unified data foundation across both ESRS and IFRS disclosure requirements.

Metrics Coverage

In the Manage Metrics application, organizations can now access SAP-provided metrics mapped to IFRS S1 and S2 data points.

As of July 2026, SAP Sustainability Control Tower includes:

  • 24 quantitative metrics for IFRS S2 Climate-Related Disclosures, covering greenhouse gas emissions (Scope 1, 2, and 3) and carbon credits..
  • 57 qualitative metrics across IFRS S1 and S2, including:
    • IFRS S1 General Disclosures: 13 metrics
    • IFRS S1 and IFRS S2 shared General Disclosures: 15 metrics
    • IFRS S2 Climate-Related Disclosures: 29 metrics

To support interoperability, qualitative metrics that appear in both IFRS S1 and IFRS S2 are represented as a single metric where applicable. This avoids duplicating data entry for disclosures that serve both standards.

Data points that include explanatory content, implementation guidance, or reporting clarifications are out of scope and are not included in the delivered master data. Organizations can refer to the corresponding IFRS standards documentation for this information.

Within metric disclosure references, customers can see, when applicable, interoperability between disclosure requirements across standards such as ESRS and IFRS, helping organizations address multiple reporting frameworks through a single reporting effort.

Reporting Scope

Once metrics are available, organizations define their reporting scope: which material metrics apply to which parts of their reporting structure, and for which periods. The reporting scope configuration in SAP Sustainability Control Tower allows organizations to select IFRS-relevant metrics alongside their existing ESRS scope.

This is particularly relevant for organizations reporting under both ESRS and IFRS, where many climate-related disclosures overlap. Because SAP Sustainability Control Tower supports interoperability with standards in its metric definitions, organizations can configure their reporting scope once and satisfy data requirements for multiple frameworks without managing parallel reporting processes.

Data Acquisition

With the reporting scope defined, data acquisition follows established SAP Sustainability Control Tower patterns. Quantitative data for IFRS S2 climate metrics can be sourced through the same channels used for ESRS climate data:

  • SAP Sustainability Footprint Management integration for greenhouse gas emissions data across scope 1, 2 and 3, with configurable period granularity (monthly, quarterly, yearly) based on the active fiscal year variant.
  • SAP Sustainability Footprint Management energy and fuel mix metrics for IFRS‑relevant climate information, adding energy consumption, energy mix, fuel mix, energy content and sourcing type, calculated together with the emissions in a single run.
  • SAP Datasphere content packages for loading actuals from connected source systems.
  • File upload for data not available through system integration.
  • Inbound ODATA API for programmatic data ingestion from any source.

For qualitative metrics, the data collection process supports form-based collection with workflow governance. Organizations can group IFRS qualitative metrics within a single data collection form, assign processors and approvers, and manage the entire collection cycle through governed workflows. It also supports collection of the corporate summary while showing previously recorded data for comparison.

Emissions and Energy Data

For IFRS S2 greenhouse gas disclosures, SAP Sustainability Footprint Management calculates corporate emissions directly based on ERP master and transactional data, rather than from manual uploads or separate data silos. This creates one governed data foundation for Scope 1, 2 and 3 results that aligns climate metrics with the same operational events and structures used to run the business.

In the same calculation flow, the solution can also deliver energy‑related metrics such as energy consumption, energy mix, fuel mix, energy content and sourcing type, where these are relevant for IFRS S2 reporting. One run of the model, therefore, produces multiple climate‑related outputs: mandatory emissions data plus optional energy metrics to support transition analysis and target tracking.

Because activity data, emission factors and calculation logic are all traceable back to their source, the resulting IFRS S2 figures are easier to substantiate. When transferred into SAP Sustainability Control Tower via direct integration, this governance and lineage are preserved, connecting operational cause, calculation and disclosure in a single, auditable chain - eliminating the need for external transformation or aggregation steps.

Note: SAP Sustainability Control Tower's emissions-related Data Provider Interfaces (including SAP_SCT_DPI_Emission and SAP_SCT_DPI_Energy) provide the structure for ingesting this data from SAP and non-SAP sources.

Analytics

Analyze ESG Data application enables organizations to explore their IFRS-aligned sustainability performance. With the target-versus-actual visualization capabilities delivered in Q2 2026, organizations can compare their climate-related performance against defined targets directly within the application.

For IFRS S2, this is particularly relevant for transition plan monitoring: organizations can track progress toward their greenhouse gas reduction targets, visualize emissions trends across reporting periods, and drill down by reporting structure to identify areas requiring attention.

The flexible chart-specific configuration model allows independent control over metric selection, filters, drilldowns, and chart types. Users can display up to six charts in parallel and combine chart and table views for side-by-side analysis of different IFRS disclosure dimensions, such as comparing Scope 1, 2, and 3 emissions performance across legal entities or time periods.

 

AI-Assisted Report Generation

Generate Sustainability Reports with AI is available free of charge to all SAP Sustainability Control Tower customers as a base AI feature. Today, customers can already use the Generic ESG Reporting Template to automatically draft ESG reports in multiple languages, preview and edit the content, and download the result in PDF or DOCX format, with dynamic AI‑driven revisions helping refine the final narrative.

Building on these capabilities, the Generate ESG Reports with AI application will include an IFRS‑specific report template as a roadmap item for H2 2026. This template will allow organizations to generate a structured IFRS sustainability disclosure report from the quantitative and qualitative data managed in SAP Sustainability Control Tower, drawing on the governed ESG data foundation to produce a draft aligned with IFRS S1 and S2 requirements. Using the IFRS template, AI‑assisted report generation will cover the core IFRS content pillars—Governance, Strategy, Risk Management, and Metrics and Targets—providing a standards‑aligned starting point that customers can further tailor to their specific context.

 Interoperability: ESRS and IFRS Side by Side

A key design principle behind the IFRS content in SAP Sustainability Control Tower is interoperability. Organizations reporting under both ESRS (for CSRD compliance) and IFRS (for jurisdictions adopting ISSB standards) can manage both from a single data foundation.

Where disclosure requirements overlap, particularly in general disclosures and climate-related reporting, SAP Sustainability Control Tower's metric definitions are designed so that data entered once can satisfy requirements across both frameworks. Within the metric general information, organizations can see the mapping between ESRS and IFRS disclosure requirements, reducing the effort required to maintain parallel reporting tracks.

Additional Information

Documentation on IFRS S1 and S2 metrics is available in SAP Help Portal for SAP Sustainability Control Tower.

Scenario Guidance for Corporate Carbon Calculation is available in SAP Help Portal for SAP Sustainability Footprint Management.

Reproduction and use rights are strictly limited.  For more information about the IFRS Foundation and rights to use its material please visit www.ifrs.org. © 2026 IFRS Foundation

entry.title: Re: Can JPEG/PNG attachments be printed automatically wit...
entry.content:
Dear @tathyaprakash_sahood9d589,

Public Cloud supports automatically assigning Maintenance Order attachments to output items such as the Job Card. This is standard configuration using Assign Document Types for Output and Define Output Settings for Document Types therefore, no enhancement is required for selecting and adding the attachments

JPG/JPEG and PNG are supported by the SAP Attachment Service and can be stored and previewed as attachments. However, SAP explicitly states that only PDF documents can be merged with the output item. Other file types remain separate/additional documents and are not incorporated into the Job Card PDF

Therefore:

  • If the requirement is to create one printable Job Card document containing the attachment pages, the images must first be converted to PDF
  • PDF attachments can then be automatically selected and merged using standard configuration
  • If JPG/PNG -> PDF conversion must happen automatically, an upstream conversion process or a side-by-side SAP BTP workaround would be required. SAP does not document a released Maintenance Order output enhancement that performs this image conversion inside the standard output process

More details below:

SAP Help – Automatic Attachments for Maintenance Notification / Maintenance Order / Maintenance Job Packs

SAP Help – Print Multiple Orders and Operations

SAP Help – Manage Work Packs

SAP Help – MIME Types Supported by Attachment Service

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Inside SAP Business Data Cloud: How Enterprises Run at Scale
entry.content:
Enterprise data platforms live or die by trust. Trust that data will land where it should, when it should. Trust that when something goes wrong, someone is watching. Trust that governance, observability and lifecycle management aren’t afterthoughts, they’re built into the fabric.

SAP Business Data Cloud (SAP BDC) was designed with exactly that principle in mind. In this post, we’ll focus on the how, the concrete capabilities, controls and design decisions that make SAP BDC a platform, enterprises can confidently build their data strategy on.

A Comprehensive Strategy for Enterprise Data

SAP BDC is a comprehensive data fabric that:

  • Combines business semantics, process context, documentation and technical metadata with curated data, so it’s understandable, governed and trustworthy.
  • Standardizes access across SAP and non-SAP sources for consistent, interoperable consumption at scale.
  • Exposes data through governed interfaces so it can be reused across lines of business without replication-heavy integrations.
  • Is optimized for agentic AI, ML, and analytics scenarios, leveraging SAP’s deep understanding of business processes and metadata.

Enterprise readiness isn’t marketing language, it’s measurable. Here’s a transparent look at how SAP BDC has advanced its core platform and lifecycle capabilities with clear delivered outcomes.

1. Core Platform & Lifecycle Capabilities

🚀Provisioning & Activation, Dramatic Speed Improvements

The challenge: data product onboarding/offboarding previously ranged from hours to days, inflating project timelines and TCO.

What’s been delivered:

  • Onboarding reduced by upto 98.2% in production
  • Offboarding reduced by upto 93.7% in production

The outcome: faster time-to-value, lower project costs and the ability to iterate on data products at a pace real enterprises demand.

🔄Extensibility & Schema Evolution, No More Downtime for Schema Changes

The challenge: Historically, schema changes on data products or lines of business applications (adding a field, adding a data source, etc.) required customers to offboard and re-onboard a data product, causing painful downtime.

What’s been delivered:

  • Semantic Versioning and Schema Evolution for SAP managed data products, enabling smoother updates and backward compatibility
  • Support for both breaking and non-breaking schema changes
  • Data reload functionality for SAP managed data products

The outcome: enterprises can evolve their data models continuously, schema changes without downtime is exactly what production-grade platforms should deliver.

🎛️ Data Product Lifecycle, Granular Control, Fewer Surprises

The challenge:

  • Lack of pre-deployment validation led to failures during data product activation
  • Missing granular controls caused downtime in other data products and Intelligent Content
  • No notifications in the SAP BDC cockpit for available data product updates

What’s been delivered:

  • Granular controls to install/update/uninstall an individual data product
  • Pre-deployment validation checks for data products released to block installations with missing dependencies
  • Customer notification via the SAP BDC Cockpit when new versions of SAP-managed data products become available.
  • Compatibility checks for Analytic Content Network (ACN) packages with SAP Datasphere/SAP Analytics Cloud

The outcome: fewer failed deployments, isolated impact when changes occur and proactive awareness of what’s available.

2. Built-In Observability and Monitoring

The challenge: Enterprise readiness isn’t just about deployment, it is also about what happens after.

Teams need real-time visibility into pipelines, data flows, and system health.

What’s been delivered: SAP BDC ships with a dedicated monitoring experience in the SAP BDC Cockpit for administrators and data engineers, providing:

  • Pipeline visibility: intelligent content installations, data package activations, and system connectivity
  • Data Flow Monitor: inspection of acquisition and transformation phases with input/output/dropped record metrics
  • Two focused views: Objects to Monitor (active issues) and Installed Objects (successful deployments and ongoing health)
  • Consistent status semantics: success / warning / error giving teams a predictable diagnostic language

 

The outcome: Every data product is versioned, and "Update Available" indicators surface when new SAP-delivered updates are ready, so customers stay current without disruptive migrations.

3. Governance and Trust by Design

The challenge: Governance bolted on after the fact never scales, it has to be embedded from day one.

With SAP BDC, Governance is embedded, not bolted on:

  • Data Governance: master data management, data quality and stewardship
  • Catalog and Lineage: every data product is traceable and discoverable
  • Knowledge Core: mission-critical business context traveling with the data
  • Semantics layer: business definitions, KPIs, and active metadata baked in

The outcome: data that is understandable, governed and trustworthy by design, ready to be reused across lines of business without replication-heavy integrations.

What’s Next?

Enterprise readiness is not a finish line for SAP BDC; it’s a journey of continuous hardening and optimization. We’ll keep extending provisioning speed, lifecycle control and observability with a clear roadmap so enterprises can move even faster with confidence.

The Takeaway

SAP BDC is enterprise-ready because the capabilities that matter aren’t promises, they’re delivered, measured, and continuously improving:

✅ Provisioning is upto 50–60× faster: from days to minutes
✅ Schema evolution without downtime
✅ Granular lifecycle controls: install, update, uninstall individual data products safely
✅ Pre-deployment validation: catch dependency issues before they cause failures
✅ End-to-end observability: from source connectivity to transformed data products
✅ Native governance, semantics and lineage

entry.title: Re: SMARTRECUITERS FOR SAP Successfactors
entry.content:
@Nadeemahmed_6 

Welcome to the community, and there's nothing dumb about your question, self-studying SF modules without a sandbox is genuinely one of the hardest parts of breaking into this space, especially on the functional/implementation side rather than the recruiting side you already know well.

On THR97 / Onboarding: Since you've already done THR83, you're building on solid ground ONB shares a lot of conceptual overlap with RCM. Keep working through the SAP Learning Hub exercises if you have access; if not, the openSAP courses and the public SF Community wiki pages for ONB 1.0/2.0 have walkthroughs that don't require a live system.

On practice systems: Your biggest bottleneck is going to be system access, since SF (and SmartRecruiters) don't offer public trial tenants (for development) the way some other software does. A few paths people typically use:

  • SAP Learning Hub subscription (if your employer or a training provider can sponsor it) - gives you access to provisioned practice systems tied to the official courses.
  • Partner/implementation firms sometimes give consultants-in-training sandbox access, worth networking with SF partners even informally.
  • QUICK NOTE:  if you happen to be with an SF/SmartRecruiters partner organization, you can request access to the SmartRecruiters demo environment directly here:
  • https://smartrecruiters-approuter.cfapps.us10.hana.ondemand.com/request-access
      Good luck! Keep at it, this stuff clicks over time.

entry.title: Help with Service Layer - Invoices
entry.content:
Hello everyone,

My name is Eduardo López del Castillo. I'm a developer working for a company in Mexico that uses SAP Business One. Currently, I'm developing a web application that integrates with SAP Business One through the Service Layer.

My backend is built with FastAPI, and it communicates directly with the SAP Business One Service Layer.

I'm currently facing an issue when creating A/R Invoices through the Service Layer. I can create the invoice successfully using a Sales Quotation as the base document, but the problem appears when I try to generate the CFDI (electronic invoice) for the Mexican SAT.

In other words, I'm trying to trigger the Electronic File Manager (EFM) from the Service Layer, but it always fails, even though I'm sending the same fields that I see in invoices created directly from the SAP Business One client.

Here is a simplified version of the payload I'm sending to my backend:

payload = {
    "CardCode": cardcode,
    "DocDate": today,
    "DocDueDate": today,
    "TaxDate": data.tax_date or today,
    "PaymentGroupCode": payment_group_code,
    "PaymentMethod": PAYMENT_METHODS_OPYM.get(pm, ""),
    "U_B1SYS_MainUsage": invoice_data.get("cfdi_uso") or "S01",
    "U_CP": invoice_data.get("cp_fiscal") or cp,
    "FederalTaxID": invoice_data.get("lictradnum") or "XAXX010101000",
    "U_TRANSPORTES": invoice_data.get("reg_fiscal") or "616",
    "U_fpago": invoice_data.get("forma_pago") or ("PPD" if pm == "POR DEFINIR" else "PUE"),
    "EDocGenerationType": "edgt_Generate",
    "U_MetPago": "01",
    "ShipToCode": "DIR",
    "PayToCode": "DIR",
    "AddressExtension": {
        "ShipToZipCode": invoice_data.get("cp_fiscal") or cp,
        "BillToZipCode": invoice_data.get("cp_fiscal") or cp,
    },
    "ElectronicProtocols": [
        {
            "ProtocolCode": "edpc_CFDI",
            "GenerationType": "edgt_Generate",
            "MappingID": 27,
            "TestingMode": "tNO",
            "PaymentMethod": "01",
            "CFDiExport": "01",
        }
    ],
    "DocumentLines": document_lines,
}

The overall flow of my endpoint is:

  1. Retrieve the complete Sales Quotation information.

  2. Create the A/R Invoice (without payment).

    • I initially had another issue here: I had to set the PaymentGroupCode to -1 (Cash Basic) because SAP wouldn't allow me to create the invoice and register the payment at the same time.

  3. Attempt to generate the CFDI (currently disabled while testing).

  4. Create the Incoming Payment for the invoice.

  5. Finish the process.

The invoice and payment are created successfully, but the electronic invoice generation always fails.

The error message is always the same and provides very little information:

Can not call the Electronic File Manager

That's all the Service Layer returns.

I've already tried sending all the electronic document fields that I could find from invoices generated through the SAP Business One client, but I still receive the same error.

Has anyone experienced this issue while implementing SAP Business One Service Layer integrations?

Is there any additional configuration required for the Electronic File Manager when creating invoices through the Service Layer?

Any advice or suggestions would be greatly appreciated.

Thank you!

entry.title: Re: Ledger setup public cloud finance
entry.content:
Hello!

Assigning a country-specific GAAP to 0L at ledger level is possible in SSCUI 103556, and having local GAAPs at company-code level in 2L doesn't trigger the FINS_ACDOC_CUST499 check, because that check only requires that no more than one ledger has CoCode-level local AP assignments, which would be 2L alone.

An accounting principle assigned at ledger level applies to all company codes in that ledger, and all company codes in the system must be assigned to 0L. So a local GAAP on 0L forces every company code, including foreign ones, under that one country's GAAP in the leading ledger. The KBA (v36, Scenario 2, "Important to Know" point 5) now addresses this explicitly: do not use a local accounting principle as the corporate accounting principle on ledger level for 0L, even if you only have company codes from one country today, because with that setting no company code from another country can be activated in the future in such a system.

Since your design involves other local GAAPs in 2L, those foreign company codes couldn't be activated. The country-specific book/tax depreciation areas in Asset Accounting for those countries also deploy when the local AP sits at company-code level with no AP at ledger level in the local ledger. The only case where "local GAAP on 0L" is viable is a permanently single-country system.

The supported design: corporate AP (IFRS or USGP) on 0L at ledger level, and all local GAAPs, including the home country's at company-code level in one local ledger (2L or 3L). If group reports under a local GAAP, this needs to be raised with SAP during pre-scoping (see SAP Note 3726924), since the ledger and AP decisions can't be corrected later without reprovisioning.

entry.title: Re: In the field catalog of the condition Table, Which "R...
entry.content:
Hello!

Per SAP Note 3337056, the Supplier's Region in purchasing pricing is carried by the field WKREG (labeled "Region of Dlv. Plant" / PlantRegion), which is filled from the supplier's or goods supplier's address region (LFA1-REGIO). The receiving/procuring plant's region is carried by the field REGIO (labeled simply "Region"), which is filled from the plant's address as maintained in "Define Plant" in CBC (T001W-REGIO). The corresponding country fields follow the same pattern: ALAND holds the supplier's country and LAND1 holds the receiving plant's country. The fields REGIOA (Departure region) and REGIOZ (Destination region) should not be used for purchase order pricing, as they belong to the shipment and foreign-trade context.

The pricing communication structure fields follow the goods-flow direction, sender versus recipient, not the party names. In purchasing, the supplier is the sender, so the supplier's region lands in WKREG; the plant is the recipient, so the plant's region lands in REGIO.

entry.title: Re: Material BOM Migration (2602) - BOM Item Document Ass...
entry.content:
Hello!

The document key was supplied in the wrong worksheet. For a category-D item, the document key (Document Type, Document, Document Version, Document Part) is the identifying data of the BOM item itself and must be maintained directly in the BOM Item worksheet, in the document key columns of that item's row, it takes the place of the component number that a stock item would have. The separate "BOM Item Document Assignment" worksheet serves a different purpose: it creates document assignments (DMS object links) that attach Document Info Records to existing BOM items as additional references. It does not supply the identifying key of a document item. The item record for Item ID 00000002 reached the posting logic with item category D but an empty document key, which raises message 356 regardless of the content of the assignment sheet. Manual assignment works because in the app the key is entered on the item directly.

Resolution: In the BOM Item worksheet, on the row for Item ID 00000002, maintain Document Type ZBO, Document 10000000003, Document Version A, and Document Part 000, leave the component/material field empty, keep Item Category D, and maintain a quantity (typically 1). Remove the corresponding row from the BOM Item Document Assignment worksheet, or reserve that sheet for attaching additional documents to component items. Re-run the simulation.

Supporting measures: Format the Document and Document Part columns as text so that Excel does not drop leading zeros ("000" must not become "0") or convert the document number to a numeric value. After a successful simulation, verify one migrated BOM in the Maintain Bill of Material app to confirm the document item appears with the correct DIR reference.

Escalation path: If the BOM Item worksheet of the 2602 template genuinely contains no document key columns, meaning the object structurally could not create category-D items, this could be a content gap in the migration object. In that case, raise a customer incident, referencing the migration object "Material BOM," release 2602, and message 29 356.

entry.title: Re: Applicability of Alternative FYV in a 2SL Environment
entry.content:
Answer 1, ledger assignment: Per SAP Note 3169537, only one common fiscal year variant is allowed in the leading ledger 0L, which is mandatory for all company codes. An Alternative FYV can only be assigned at company-code level to the non-leading, parallel ledger (e.g., 2L or 3L). The supporting ledger is the standard design: corporate accounting principle at ledger level on 0L, local accounting principles at company-code level in the non-leading ledger, where the AFYV and its posting period variant are then assigned via configuration activity "Assign Fiscal Year Variants to Ledgers and Company Codes".

Answer 2, landscape question: A 3-system landscape is not a prerequisite. Note 3169537 contains no landscape-based restriction; the AFYV capability works in both 2SL and 3SL. The landscape only determines where the "no postings" prerequisite is verified (2SL: quality and production systems; 3SL: development and production systems).

Prerequisites and restrictions to validate for the new company codes:

  • A non-leading ledger must already exist in system, ledgers were fixed at initial scoping and can’t be added afterwards. Without a parallel ledger, an AFYV is not possible.
  • The AFYV can only be assigned to company codes with no postings anywhere in the landscape; assign it before any posting, including data migration postings.
  • The AFYV's posting period start and end dates must match those of the common FYV, with periods of equal length, shifted fiscal years (e.g., April–March) are supported; 4-4-5 and 13-period variants are not.
  • Postings to special periods are not supported across the ledgers of an AFYV company code, reflect this in the year-end close design.
  • Period-end closing must be executed per ledger (and for some jobs per ledger and company code); key-date-based jobs such as foreign currency valuation can run across all ledgers.

Note 3169537 remains the binding reference and is subject to change, recheck it at project start.

Below are similar topics already addressed:

https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-resource-planning-q-a/group-reporting-two-tenants-with-different-fyv-4-4-5-and-k4/qaq-p/14400975

entry.title: Securing a CAP Node.js App with SAP AMS on BTP Cloud Foundry
entry.content:
Overview
 
This tutorial walks through building a CAP Node.js application, adding role-based access control using SAP Authorization Management Service (AMS) and SAP IAS, and deploying it to SAP BTP Cloud Foundry with MTA.
 
Prerequisites
  • SAP CAP SDK (@sap/cds-dk) installed globally
  • Cloud Foundry CLI (cf) installed
  • Access to an SAP BTP subaccount with:
    • SAP HANA Cloud instance
    • SAP IAS (Identity Authentication Service) tenant
    • SAP AMS (Authorization Management Service) entitlement
    • SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition (optional)

 

Procedure

 

Step 1 — Initialize the CAP Project

 

Create a new CAP Node.js project and add the built-in bookshop sample data model:
cds init capams --nodejs
cd capams
cds add sample

 
Step 2 — Add Authorization Annotations

 

Open srv/admin-service.cds and annotate the Books and Authors entities with @restrict to define role-based access:
annotate AdminService.Books with @(restrict: [
  {
    grant: ['READ'],
    to   : 'ManageAuthors'
  },
  {
    grant: ['READ', 'WRITE'],
    to   : 'ManageBooks'
  }
]);

annotate AdminService.Authors with @(restrict: [
  {
    grant: ['READ', 'WRITE'],
    to   : 'ManageAuthors'
  },
  {
    grant: ['READ'],
    to   : 'ManageBooks'
  }
]);

 

  • ManageAuthors can read/write Authors but only read Books
  • ManageBooks can read/write Books but only read Authors

 
Step 3 — Add IAS Authentication and AMS Authorization

 

Add SAP IAS as the authentication provider and SAP AMS for fine-grained authorization:
cds add ias
cds add ams

 

cds add ias adds "auth": "ias" under the [production] profile in package.json. cds add ams adds the @SAP/ams-dev dev dependency and wires up the AMS plugin.

 

Step 4 — Add HANA Cloud, MTA, and Work Zone Support
Configure SAP HANA Cloud as the production database, generate the MTA deployment descriptor, and add SAP Build Work Zone integration:
cds add hana
cds add mta
cds add workzone

 

cds add hana sets "model": "db/hana" for the production profile. cds add mta generates mta.yaml wiring all modules and service bindings. cds add workzone adds destination, html5-repo, workzone, and workzone-standard facets.

 

Step 5 — Deploy to Cloud Foundry

 

Log in to your BTP Cloud Foundry environment using SSO, then deploy the full MTA:
cf login -a <cloud-foundry-api-url> --sso
cds up

 

cds up compiles the AMS policies from the @restrict annotations, builds the MTA archive, and deploys all modules and services. Once complete, you will find the generated basePolicies.dcl under gen/policies/dcl/cap/:
POLICY "admin" {
    ASSIGN ROLE "admin";
}

POLICY "ManageAuthors" {
    ASSIGN ROLE "ManageAuthors";
}

POLICY "ManageBooks" {
    ASSIGN ROLE "ManageBooks";
}

 

Step 6 — Verify the Deployment

 

Service Bindings in BTP Cockpit

 

In the BTP Cockpit, navigate to your Cloud Foundry space, open the capams-srv application, and go to Service Bindings. Confirm that capams-ias (identity service, application plan) is bound.

 

 

Authorization Policies in SAP IAS

 

In the SAP IAS admin console, go to Applications & Resources → Applications, find the capams application, and open the Authorization Policies tab. The three policies generated from your annotations — admin, ManageAuthors, and ManageBooks — should appear under the cap package.
 

 

 

Click the admin policy to view its details and manage user assignments. You can assign users directly here.
 

 

 

 

 

 

Summary

 

Command Purpose

cds init capams --nodejsScaffold a new CAP Node.js project
cds add sampleAdd built-in bookshop sample data model
cds add iasConfigure IAS as the authentication provider
cds add amsAdd SAP AMS for authorization
cds add hanaSwitch production database to SAP HANA Cloud
cds add mtaGenerate the MTA deployment descriptor
cds add workzoneAdd SAP Build Work Zone integration
cds upBuild and deploy the full MTA to Cloud Foundry

 

The End! Thanks for your time! Jacky Liu

entry.title: Q2 2026 Innovations: SAP Enterprise Service Management
entry.content:
Q2 went by fast! I hope that you all were able to participate in SAPPHIRE 2026 either in person or virtually. The SAP Autonomous Enterprise was announced and a shout out of huge thanks to our service customers Pfizer and Doehler for sharing your incredible stories & insights about your transformation, AI adoption and value outcomes with SAP Service Cloud and SAP ESM! Our next value-add event is queued up -  SAP Connect 2026 is coming to Las Vegas, October 5-7 and we welcome our customers and partners to secure an early bird rate by August 10th to discover how to maximize value of your solutions, explore best practices in AI and business transformation approaches and much, much more!

Before I jump into our Q2 innovations for SAP ESM there are a couple of exciting opportunities I’d like to share.

  • It’s not too late to join our SAP ESM webcast on Tuesday, July 21st, focused on HR Service Delivery: AI, Data, and Experience: From Transactions to Intelligent HR Services also featuring a widely recognized expert Anita Lettink. If you’re reading post-event, click the link above and watch the replay!
  • Also, there is a new SAP Learning module for SAP ESM: Integrating SAP Enterprise Service Management. This course is available at no cost and is designed specifically for administrators, consultants, and business users to deepen their knowledge of SAP Enterprise Service Management. This course focuses on integration configuration to enable execution of end-to-end scenarios delivering true value and operational efficiency across the organization.

Now back to the focus of this blog – our Q2 innovations for SAP ESM! There are so many innovations and improvements please register for our Q2 Innovation webcast July 29th hosted by our product team.

Self-Service Catalog

The Self-Service Catalog provides employees with a guided, intuitive way to find and request the services they need, without requiring an understanding of the underlying processes or systems. Requests submitted through the appropriate topic catalog or tile are automatically resolved or routed based on the inquiry context, which reduces manual triage effort, frustration in solutioning and shortens resolution times. For service agents, this means fewer incomplete or misrouted cases and more time spent on higher-value work. For employees, it means a simpler, more predictable experience when engaging with their enterprise or shared service teams.

Service Catalog configuration

                                                                                             

Service Catalog Self Service

 

Content Package Framework

The Content Package Framework enables organizations to deploy SAP ESM more quickly by leveraging prebuilt, reusable configurations for case types, workflows, and catalogs. Rather than building service processes from the ground up, customers and SI partners can start from proven templates and adapt them to specific business needs. This reduces implementation effort, simplifies rollout across lines of business, and helps organizations standardize service delivery at scale. Content packages for HR service delivery are planned for a future release.

New Email Editor

The new email editor provides service agents with a modern, streamlined interface for composing, editing, and managing customer communications. Designed with an AI-first architecture in mind, the editor supports faster email creation while maintaining the quality and consistency expected in service interactions. Coupled with the AI email drafter capability, agents truly spend less time formatting and more time focused on resolving customer issues, which improves response times and communication quality.

 

Email editor

Creation of Sales Objects from the Customer Hub

While this capability is more closely aligned with SAP Service Cloud, sales support and B2B supplier support are common use cases we see deployed for SAP ESM as well. With that said, service agents can now create and manage leads, opportunities, appointments, and sales orders directly from the service agent desktop when the interaction calls for it, reducing clicks and upskilling time. The capability supports closer alignment between service and sales teams and delivers that seamless experience you expect between SAP solutions.

 Self-Service Enhancements

Two enhancements provide administrators with greater control over the self-service experience:

  • Edit Form in Self-Service During Case Lifecycle feature allows administrators to define conditions under which a form remains editable, giving employees the ability to update information at appropriate points in the case lifecycle without agent involvement.
  • Adapt Case Details Screen per Case Type feature allows administrators to configure the Case Details screen in Self-Service on a per-case-type basis, ensuring that users only see fields and actions that are relevant to their specific scenario.

Together, these enhancements help drive higher self-service adoption and reduce unnecessary agent handoffs.

Embedded Component for Custom Services

Administrators can now define list views or object forms within a custom service and embed them into custom user interfaces, as well as selected standard UIs. By mapping parameters from the host page, related custom records can be viewed and worked on directly within the relevant UI view. This capability makes it easier to extend SAP ESM to fit unique business processes and ensures that agents and users have access to contextual information exactly where they need it, without navigating away from their current task.

New Self-Service Experience (Restricted Release)

A new Self-Service experience for SAP ESM is being introduced as a restricted release. Aligned with the latest self-service catalog capabilities, the new experience is designed to support cross-functional deployment across the enterprise, providing instant solutions to common inquiries and enabling contextual case creation and management. As this is a restricted release, customers interested in exploring the capability are encouraged to request access via the solution. Early feedback from customers will play an important role in shaping the general availability release, and participation is strongly encouraged.

Sign In as Another User

Administrators can now sign in as another business user seeing the same functionality and authorizations as that user. This allows administrators to reproduce and troubleshoot issues from the user’s perspective without screen sharing or password exchange. This results in faster issue resolution, stronger security practices, and a more efficient support process for both administrators and end users.

Analytics Improvements

This release delivers two important improvements to analytics capabilities:

  • Embedded SAC Widgets allow SAP Analytics Cloud visualizations to be embedded directly into the SAP ESM interface, displaying focused analytics with individual tiles within the daily flow of various business objects such as cases, customers and opportunities. These widgets can also be incorporated into mashups for more comprehensive analytics overall.
  • Custom Service Entities in Analytics allow custom service entities to be used as data sources in SAP Analytics Cloud. Once a custom entity is marked as Analytics Relevant, the system automatically generates a corresponding data source that appears under Custom Data Sources in the Query Designer, where it can be combined with standard data sources to produce tailored analytics content.

These enhancements bring insights closer to where work happens and reduce the need to switch between systems for data-driven decision-making.

Approval Process Simplification

Two enhancements to approval processes help organizations improve consistency and reduce delays.

  • Configure Approval and Rejection Reasons: Administrators can now configure predefined approval and rejection reasons, create custom reason lists, assign them to specific approval processes, and control whether reason selection is mandatory or optional. This supports more consistent decision-making and improves auditability across approval workflows.
  • Automatic Approval Settings: Configuration allowing approval steps to be automatically approved or rejected if no action is taken within a defined duration can be defined from one hour to ten days. This helps ensure that workflows continue to progress even when approvers are unavailable, reducing bottlenecks and shortening overall case cycle times.

Accelerating Case Resolution with UX Simplicity and Automation

Several enhancements in this release focus on simplifying the case experience and giving administrators more configuration control. A few examples include:

  • Case Data Retention Rules by Case Type: This feature allows administrators to configure retention rules for automated transaction depersonalization on a per-case-type basis, helping the organization meet legal, regional, and business-specific data retention requirements.
  • Multiple Catalogs Mapped to One Case Type: The ability to map multiple catalogs to one case type allows administrators to assign additional category catalogs to a case type, minimizing configuration effort where similar case types can be used across various business functions.
  • Hide Category Levels in Case: This feature allows administrators to selectively hide specific category levels within cases, providing agents with a cleaner and more focused interface by removing unnecessary hierarchical information.

Case adaptation

These enhancements help streamline the agent experience while giving administrators the flexibility to align case management with compliance, business, and operational requirements.

Useful Information

For additional Q2 innovations relevant for SAP Enterprise Service Management please reference Kirsi Tarvainen’s blog on SAP Service Cloud and register for the upcoming Q2 Release Webinar. To stay up-to-date as we release capabilities each month and to get a full view of what’s new for Q2, reference the What’s New page in our help portal. Also, reference these valuable links for more on SAP ESM and SAP CX solutions:

SAP CX Q2 2020 Innovation News

SAP Learning ESM Course

SAP Service Cloud V2 Certification Course

SAP Release Navigator

SAP ESM’s free trial Get hands-on experience today with an HR service delivery scenario (Finance shared services coming soon).

Use the SAP Enterprise Service Management managed tag when posting on SAP ESM topics in the community!

And finally, thank you. The continued growth of this community, and the willingness of its members to share, question, and collaborate, is what moves SAP ESM forward. Every SAP employee, partner, and customer benefits from the collective knowledge exchanged here and it’s genuinely appreciated.

entry.title: Transport sequencing does not account for object-level dependencies between TRs within same Feature
entry.content:
Dear SAP,

We are using SAP Cloud ALM Features for change and transport management on our SAP landscape.

Within a single Feature, we can have multiple Transport Requests (TRs) assigned. In our observation, when these TRs are released and imported, the sequence follows the order in which the TRs were created (and consequently released into the import buffer) — not any logical or functional dependency between the objects contained in them.

Scenario:

  • TR1 is created first, and contains an object that is functionally dependent on an object delivered in TR2 (created later).
  • TR2 is created after TR1 and therefore is released/imported after TR1, following the standard "release in creation order" behavior.
  • Because TR1 imports before TR2, the object in TR1 that depends on the not-yet-imported object in TR2 could fail activation, throw an error, or behave inconsistently at import time — since its prerequisite object is not yet present in the target system.

Questions :

  1. Does Cloud ALM Feature functionality have any built-in mechanism to detect or account for object-level dependencies between TRs within the same Feature, prior to release/import?
  2. If such object dependencies exist across TRs in the same Feature, is there a supported way to control or override the import sequence (independent of TR creation order) to ensure dependent objects import in the correct order?
  3. If no automated mechanism exists, what is SAP's recommended best practice for identifying and managing such intra-Feature object dependencies before release, to avoid import failures or inconsistent object states in the target system?
  4. We understand Transport Checks (Downgrade Protection / Cross-Reference Check) are available, but these are documented as supported only against the production system. Can these (or an equivalent check) be leveraged, or are there plans to extend them, to validate object dependencies before the QA import stage, where this kind of conflict would first surface?

Regards,


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: Transport sequencing does not account for object-leve...
entry.content:
Hi ,
Thank you for the description of the scenario. We have analyzed the behavior you observed and would like to share our findings, organized by your original questions.
SAP Cloud ALM triggers native TMS "tp" commands on the managed system to perform imports. The specific command depends on the number of Transport Requests (TRs) involved:
  • When a Feature has a single TR, a “tp IMPORT” command is triggered for that TR alone.
  • When a Feature has multiple TRs — or when multiple Features are deployed together via the Deployment Scheduler — a “tp IMPORT SUBSET” command is triggered instead.
“tp IMPORT SUBSET” imports the TRs strictly in the order they appear in the target system's import buffer, i.e., their release order. This is native TMS behavior, and SAP Cloud ALM does not intervene in or override this sequence. This confirms your observation: the import order you are seeing is expected behavior, not a defect.
The import sequence cannot be changed or altered if the import is triggered by SAP Cloud ALM Features or the Features app.
if you really feel that you need to change the import sequence of Transport Requests, the only option is:
  1. Unassign the relevant Transport Requests from any SAP Cloud ALM Features that they are assigned to.
  2. Manually remove all the relevant Transport Requests from the import queue of the target system:client.
  3. Manually add the removed Transport Requests to the import queue of the target system:client again, this time in the desired import sequence.
  4. Assign the Transport Requests to SAP Cloud ALM Features.
Please note, it is not sufficient to simply unassign a Transport Request from a Feature with lots of Transport Requests, and then assign it to a Feature where the Transport Request will be alone. In the short term, you will be able to control the import sequence by performing several individual Feature deployments (e.g. triggering several "tp IMPORT" commands in the right sequence).
But this could cause problems downstream. If a mass deployment is later performed by selecting multiple Features and using the option to schedule their deployment in SAP Cloud ALM, this will trigger the "tp IMPORT SUBSET" command. So the actual import buffer sequence will be used to import the Transport Requests, thus breaking your manual import sequence efforts. To avoid this, please ensure that the above procedure is followed in full so that the real import sequence is adjusted before any involvement by SAP Cloud ALM Features.
Best regards,
Leo

entry.title: Re: Unable to access Local Tables using Space Database Us...
entry.content:
Hi @KAMAL_1806,

OpenSQL Schema can only consume reporting-enabled objects: Views with flag "Expose for Consumption" as on.
See SAP Datasphere help page Consume Data in Power BI and Other Clients, Tools, and Apps via ODBC/JDBC.
This is also documented in KBA 3469992 - Database user on Space-level is not able to consume tables and all schemas in SAP Datasphere.

If you want to expose local table's data,  "wrap" these objects with a view with 1:1 mapping, then enable "Expose for Consumption". 

BR.

entry.title: Re: How to to consume SA) datasphere data in on prem HANA
entry.content:

Yes, that's the correct documentation 👍

entry.title: Re: MCP Servers on SAP Integration Suite : Build Your First MCP Server from an API
entry.content:
Hi @AlexDong 

In the current implementation, the MCP Server authenticates the calling client/application (for example, Cline or another MCP client) using OAuth credentials, and authorization is enforced based on the configured policy.

Thanks,
Ashutosh
